behaviour.mcq: Select one option. Answer revealed immediately on selection.

questions.stem: The layers of the epidermis from deep to superficial are:
questions.explanation: From the basement membrane upward: stratum basale → spinosum → granulosum → (lucidum) → corneum. Keratinocytes mature and flatten as they ascend, forming the protective cornified layer.

questions.stem: The pigment-producing cell of the epidermis, located in the basal layer, is the:
questions.explanation: Melanocytes (neural-crest derived, basal layer) make melanin in melanosomes and transfer it to keratinocytes to shield nuclear DNA from UV. Langerhans cells are antigen-presenting; Merkel cells mediate touch.

questions.stem: Allergic contact dermatitis is an example of which hypersensitivity reaction?
questions.explanation: ACD is a type IV delayed hypersensitivity, sensitising over ~48–72 h and diagnosed by patch testing. Common allergens: nickel, fragrances, rubber, para-phenylenediamine.

questions.stem: A herald patch followed days later by a “Christmas-tree” distribution of oval scaly plaques on the trunk suggests:
questions.explanation: Pityriasis rosea: a single “herald patch” precedes an eruption following skin cleavage lines (“Christmas-tree” on the back). It is self-limiting over 6–8 weeks; systemic retinoids are not indicated.

questions.stem: Wickham striae — fine white lines on the surface of violaceous flat-topped papules — are characteristic of:
questions.explanation: Lichen planus: the “6 Ps” — pruritic, purple, polygonal, planar (flat-topped) papules/plaques — with lacy white Wickham striae; may involve oral mucosa and nails, and shows the Koebner phenomenon.

questions.stem: Pityriasis (tinea) versicolor is caused by:
questions.explanation: Overgrowth of commensal Malassezia yeast produces hypo-/hyperpigmented scaly macules on the trunk; KOH shows a “spaghetti-and-meatballs” pattern of hyphae and spores.

questions.stem: On Wood’s light examination, which finding is expected in erythrasma (Corynebacterium minutissimum)?
questions.explanation: Erythrasma fluoresces coral-red (coproporphyrin). Some scalp Microsporum tineas fluoresce green; vitiligo shows accentuated depigmentation; pseudomonas may fluoresce green.

questions.stem: Which is the classic morphology of molluscum contagiosum?
questions.explanation: Molluscum (a poxvirus) causes pearly, umbilicated papules; common in children and in HIV. Anogenital warts (HPV) are verrucous; “target” lesions suggest erythema multiforme.

questions.stem: Shingles (herpes zoster) is characterised by:

options.A: A vesicular eruption confined to a single dermatome

questions.explanation: Zoster = reactivation of varicella-zoster virus in a sensory ganglion → painful dermatomal vesicles. Complications include post-herpetic neuralgia and, occasionally, segmental motor weakness.

questions.stem: Impetigo is most commonly caused by:
questions.explanation: Impetigo (honey-coloured crusts) is usually due to S. aureus, sometimes S. pyogenes. Bullous impetigo is caused by staphylococcal exfoliative toxin.

questions.stem: A well-demarcated, tender, spreading erythema with a raised advancing edge on the lower leg, with fever, suggests:
questions.explanation: Erysipelas = superficial dermal streptococcal infection with a sharply demarcated, raised border (deeper, less-defined = cellulitis). Systemic upset is common; treat with penicillin-class antibiotics.

questions.stem: Alopecia areata is best recognised by:

options.A: Scarring patches with loss of follicular openings
options.C: Diffuse shedding 3 months after a stressor

questions.explanation: Alopecia areata = autoimmune, non-scarring round patches with tapering “exclamation-mark” hairs at the margin; nail pitting may coexist. Telogen effluvium is diffuse post-stress shedding; androgenetic alopecia causes patterned recession.

questions.stem: Telogen effluvium classically presents as:

options.A: Diffuse non-scarring hair shedding ~2–4 months after a physiological stressor
options.C: Broken hairs of irregular length from pulling

questions.explanation: A stressor (childbirth, illness, surgery, crash diet) shifts follicles synchronously into telogen; diffuse shedding follows months later and usually recovers spontaneously.

questions.stem: Which tumour is a benign vascular lesion that often bleeds and grows rapidly, frequently after minor trauma?
questions.explanation: Pyogenic granuloma (lobular capillary haemangioma) is a benign, friable, rapidly growing red nodule that bleeds easily; common in pregnancy and after trauma.

questions.stem: Seborrhoeic keratoses are:

options.A: Benign epidermal lesions with a “stuck-on” greasy appearance

questions.explanation: Seborrhoeic keratoses are common benign keratinocyte proliferations — warty, greasy, “stuck-on” pigmented papules. A sudden shower of them (Leser-Trélat sign) can rarely signal internal malignancy.

questions.stem: Regarding melanocytic naevi, which statement is correct?

options.A: They pass through junctional → compound → intradermal phases over time
options.B: They are premalignant and should all be excised

questions.explanation: Acquired naevi evolve through junctional → compound → intradermal phases as nests migrate into the dermis. Most are benign; changing or atypical lesions warrant review.

questions.stem: Stevens-Johnson syndrome / toxic epidermal necrolysis is most often triggered by:
questions.explanation: SJS/TEN is usually a severe drug reaction (sulfonamides, carbamazepine/lamotrigine, allopurinol, NSAIDs) with mucosal erosions and skin detachment. TEN = >30% body-surface detachment; Nikolsky positive.

questions.stem: The difference between SJS and TEN is based mainly on:

options.A: The percentage of body-surface-area epidermal detachment

questions.explanation: SJS <10% BSA, SJS/TEN overlap 10–30%, TEN >30% epidermal detachment. Higher detachment = higher mortality; management is supportive, often in a burns unit, with drug withdrawal.

questions.stem: Which finding favours systemic sclerosis / CREST?
questions.explanation: CREST = Calcinosis, Raynaud, oEsophageal dysmotility, Sclerodactyly, Telangiectasia (limited systemic sclerosis, anti-centromere antibody). Raynaud is often the first feature.

questions.stem: Discoid lupus erythematosus typically causes:

options.A: Scarring alopecia with atrophic, pigmented plaques

questions.explanation: DLE = chronic photosensitive plaques with follicular plugging, scaling and scarring (including scarring alopecia) and dyspigmentation. Only a minority progress to systemic lupus.

questions.stem: The commonest organism in a furuncle (boil) is:
questions.explanation: A furuncle is a deep infection of the hair follicle, almost always due to S. aureus (not Streptococcus). Coalescence of several furuncles forms a carbuncle.

questions.stem: Which statement about warts (verrucae) is correct?

options.A: They are caused by human papillomavirus
options.B: They are premalignant and should always be excised
options.C: They are caused by a poxvirus
options.D: They fluoresce coral-red under Wood’s light

questions.explanation: Common, plantar and anogenital warts are caused by HPV. Most cutaneous warts are benign and self-limiting; certain mucosal HPV types (16, 18) are oncogenic.

questions.stem: Koebner phenomenon (lesions appearing at sites of trauma) is typically seen in all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Koebnerisation occurs in psoriasis, lichen planus, vitiligo and warts, among others. BCC is a malignancy and does not koebnerise.

questions.stem: First-line topical treatment for uncomplicated scabies is:
questions.explanation: Permethrin 5% applied to the whole body is first line; treat close contacts simultaneously and launder bedding/clothing. Oral ivermectin is used for crusted or resistant cases.

questions.stem: Which best describes vitiligo?

options.A: Autoimmune destruction of melanocytes causing well-demarcated depigmented macules
options.B: A fungal infection causing scaly hypopigmented patches

questions.explanation: Vitiligo = autoimmune loss of melanocytes → sharply demarcated depigmented (chalk-white) macules that koebnerise; associated with thyroid and other autoimmune disease.

questions.stem: The most appropriate biopsy/measurement to determine melanoma prognosis is:

options.A: Excisional biopsy allowing measurement of Breslow thickness
options.B: Shave biopsy of the surface only
options.C: Punch biopsy of adjacent normal skin

questions.explanation: Suspected melanoma should have a full-thickness excisional biopsy so Breslow thickness can be measured accurately; shave biopsy risks transecting the tumour and losing depth information.

questions.stem: Which drug class is a mainstay of moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis by targeting IL-17 or IL-23?
questions.explanation: Modern psoriasis biologics target TNF-α, IL-17 (secukinumab/ixekizumab), IL-23 (guselkumab) or IL-12/23 (ustekinumab). They are highly effective for extensive disease and psoriatic arthritis.

questions.stem: A honey-coloured crusted plaque around a child’s nose and mouth is most consistent with:
questions.explanation: Impetigo: superficial staphylococcal/streptococcal infection with characteristic honey-coloured crusts, common around the nose/mouth in children; treat with topical or oral antibiotics.

questions.stem: Which is a major (essential) diagnostic criterion of atopic dermatitis?
questions.explanation: Itch (pruritus) is an essential criterion for atopic dermatitis, alongside a typical age-related distribution, chronic relapsing course and personal/family atopy.

questions.stem: Which best distinguishes cellulitis from erysipelas?

options.A: Cellulitis involves the deeper dermis/subcutis with a less well-defined border
options.B: Cellulitis has a sharply raised demarcated edge
options.C: Cellulitis is caused by a virus

questions.explanation: Cellulitis is a deeper dermal/subcutaneous infection with an ill-defined spreading border, whereas erysipelas is more superficial with a sharply raised, demarcated edge. Both are usually streptococcal/staphylococcal and can cause systemic upset.
